content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Greg Taber 
Inland Democrat 
1501 7th st., suite E 
Riverside, CA 
92507 
Dear Mr. Taber: 
August 4, 1988 
RE: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-88-285 
You have requested advice regarding the campaign disclosure 
provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974.11 
QUESTION 
What type of recipient committee is your organization and 
what are your filing requirements? 
CONCLUSION 
Your organization qualifies as a state general purpose 
recipient committee and must follow the filing schedule set 
forth in the enclosed Information Manual on Campaign Disclosure 
Provisions of the Political Reform Act (page 19) for state 
general purpose recipient committees. 
FACTS 
After recelvlng $1,000 in contributions for publishing the 
Inland Democrat newsletter, you filed the statement of 
organization (form 410). 
The newsletter, in .addition to 
providing a calendar of events of interest to Democrats in the 
RiyersidejSan Bernardino counties area, publishes the names, 
addresses and phone numbers of Democrats running for state and 
local public offices, expressly advocating their election. 
ANALYSIS 
section 82013(a) defines a recipient committee as any 
person or combination of persons who directly or indirectly 
receives contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar 
year. 
lIGovernment Code Sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
indicated. 
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code 
of Regulations section 18000, et seq. 
All references to 
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code 
of Regulations. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804*0807 • (916) 322*5660 


Taber, Greg 
Page Two 
section 82027.5 defines a general purpose recipient 
committee as a committee which is formed or exists primarily to 
support or oppose more than one candidate or ballot measure. 
section 82027.5 (b) further defines a "state" general purpose 
recipient committee as a committee to support or oppose 
candidates or measures voted on in a state election, or in more 
than one county. 
since the Inland Democrat exists to disseminate information 
pertaining to Democrats and to support various Democratic 
candidates being voted on in the 1988 state general election, 
your organization qualifies as a state general purpose 
committee. 
state general purpose recipient committees must file 
semi-annual statements for each half of the year, WHETHER OR 
NOT they have received any contributions or made any 
expenditures during the six-month period covered by the 
statement. 
Semi-annual statements are due on July 31, and 
January 31, with closing dates of June 30 and December 31, 
respectively. 
,( See the enclosed manual, page 19.) 
In addition, state general purpose recipient committees may 
be required to file up to four pre-election statements during 
an even-numbered year depending upon the level of their 
activity. 
A pre-election statement must be filed whenever the 
committee makes contributions or independent expenditures 
totaling $500 or more during the period covered by the 
pre-election statements. 
(Please review Part IV, page 32 of 
the enclosed manual for information on additional special 
reports. ) 
As we discussed on the telephone on July 25, 1988, if you 
publish any articles concerning or endorsements of a candidate 
or committee at the candidate's or committee's request or in 
coordination with the candidate or committee, you must report 
the cost of ~Ublishing the article or endorsement as an in-kind 
contributio~ to that candidate or committee. 
In addition, 
the candidate or committee on whose behalf the contribution is 
made must be informed of the fair market value of the 
contribution. 
As we discussed, one method for determining the 
~Section 82015 defines contribution as a payment for 
political purposes which is made to or "at the behest of" a 
candidate or committee. 
Regulation 18215 provides that a 
payment is made at the behest of a candidate or committee if it 
is made under the control or at the direction of, in 
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with, or at 
the request or suggestion of a candidate, controlled committee, 
official committee of a political party, or organization formed 
or existing primarily for political purposes. 


'Taber, Greg 
Page Three 
amount of the in-kind contribution is to calculate the 
percentage of the mailing space dedicated to promoting the 
candidate or committee and multiply that amount by the total 
costs involved in publishing and sending the entire 
publication, e.g. 10% x $1,000 = $100. 
If you have additional questions, please contact me at 
(916) 322-5662. 
Sincerely, 
Diane M. Grif 
General Coun 
By: 


• 
July 18, 1988 
Dianne Griffiths 
General Counsel 
F.P.P.C. 
428 J Street, Ste. 800 
Sacramento, California 95814 
Dear Ms. Griffiths, 
... . 
" 
I v I fi'f _, 
I am writing in response to the conversation we had on Friday, July 15, 
1988. 
As you know, I am the treasurer for an organization called the 
Inland Democrat Newsletter. 
Thinking that we qualified as a recipient 
committee; I filed a form 410 with the F.P.P.C. after we raised $1,000.00 
this year. 
Apparantly, the F.P.P.C. staff is not quite sure where our 
organization fits in, and you requested I send a letter to you outlining our 
publication, it's funding and it's operation. 
We have published six editions at approximately monthly intervals. 
Our 
service area is the California Democratic Party's region 15, which is 
comprised of approximately Riverside and San Bernardino Counties. 
Our 
mailing list is comprised of the membership of local Democratic Clubs, 
plus any other Democrats who are interested in receiving it. 
We mail it 
out free of charge, although we do have a voluntary subscription for 
individuals. 
We publish events of local Democratic Clubs, Committees, and Candidates, 
along with ways to contact these groups. We also try to allow a forum for 
the representatives of the region at the state party level to communicate 
with the club members, and allow a forum for the club members to express 
their concerns about internal party affairs up the line to the state party 
representatives, through letters to the editor. 
Our funding comes from a variety of sources. 
We receive contributions 
from incumbants, candidates, party central committees, party assembly 
districts, party clubs and individuals. 
We acknowledge all ce>ntributions in 
the newsletter, either with a business card reprinted or the mention of the 
contributors name. 
Of course, in the October issue we will be urging our readers to vote for 
all Democratic candidates; we took no position, however, during the 
Primary Election. 


• 
Our expenses are limited to printing and mailing costs. 
All labor required 
to write, "typeset" and administrate the newsletter is volunteer. 
We make 
no contributions to other committees or candidates. 
Please let me know, by phone if possible what kind of committee you think 
we are and just what our filing requirements are. 
I can be reached at 
(714) 683-8744. Let me know if there is any other information you require. 
I have enclosed the February and May issues for your review. 
Sincerely, 
~~­
~re~~er 
Inland Democrat Newsletter
